4: The bronze serpent incident (Num 21:4-9) occurs during one of the wilderness generation's recurring cycles of complaint-judgment-intercession-deliverance. The people speak against God and against Moses; YHWH sends fiery serpents as judgment; the people confess sin and Moses intercedes; YHWH provides the bronze serpent as a means of healing. The serpent-image was preserved in Israel and later became an object of idolatry: Hezekiah destroyed it during his reforms (2 Kings 18:4: he broke in pieces the bronze serpent that Moses had made, for until those days the people of Israel had burned incense to it). The thing appointed as a healing sign became an idol — illustrating the tendency of every divine gift to be worshiped rather than used. Jesus redeems the image by applying it to himself in John 3:14.
1: The king of Arad (a Canaanite city in the Negev) attacks Israel as it approaches from the direction of Atharim — possibly a road or pass rather than a place name. The attack catches Israel in an exposed military position. The name Arad is connected with a site in the Negev later settled by Judah (Judg 1:16-17); this episode may reflect pre-conquest contact that later became an administrative boundary. The unprovoked Canaanite aggression (Israel was not trying to enter Canaan from this direction) frames what follows as defensive war.
2: Israel makes a vow to YHWH (neder laYHWH) invoking the holy-war formula: if YHWH delivers the enemy, Israel will devote the Canaanite cities to complete destruction (ḥērem). The vow structure here is identical to the vow-law of ch30: a binding commitment to YHWH as the condition of receiving divine assistance. The ḥērem vow appears in Deuteronomy 7 and 20 as the standard prescription for Canaanite cities; here Israel voluntarily adopts the ḥērem obligation as a request rather than receiving it as a command.
3: YHWH responds to the vow — wayyišmaʿ YHWH bəqôl yiśrāʾēl — the same hearing formula as YHWH hearing Israel's cry in Egypt. The Canaanites and their cities are devoted to destruction (wayyaḥărēm ʾotām wəʾet-ʿārêhem). The place is renamed Hormah — from ḥerem (devoted destruction) — a name appearing earlier as the site of Israel's defeat (14:45), now transforming into a victory marker. The geography reverses: the place of punishment becomes the place of vindication.
5: The complaint combines theological (against God) and political (against Moses) opposition, unique in combining both objects of the complaint in one verse. The bread (leḥem) is called worthless (qəlōqēl — contemptible, nauseous) — a word used only here in the Torah. This insults the manna that YHWH provided; it is not merely complaining about lack but expressing contempt for what has been given. The complaint about the manna being disgusting provokes a different divine response than previous water-and-food complaints — the serpents rather than a plague or earthquake.
6: YHWH sends hanneḥāšîm haśśərāp̄îm — the burning/fiery serpents. שָׂרָף (śārāp̄) means burning or fiery, possibly describing the burning sensation of the venom, the appearance of the snakes, or both. The same word śərāp̄îm describes the heavenly beings in Isa 6:2 — six-winged creatures before YHWH's throne — suggesting a root meaning of glowing/burning intensity. Many Israelites die from the bites. The divine sending of serpents as judgment echoes the Eden narrative (Gen 3) and anticipates the resolution through a bronze serpent mounted on a pole.
7: The people confess: ḥāṭāʾnû kî-ḏibbarnû ḇaYHWH ûḇekā — we have sinned by speaking against YHWH and against you. The confession is specific — they name the exact sin (speaking against). Moses intercedes for them; YHWH's response does not remove the serpents but provides a healing mechanism, maintaining the judgment while opening a path of restoration. The pattern — sin, judgment, confession, provision of remedy — mirrors the Korah crisis structure but produces a different type of resolution.
8: YHWH instructs Moses to make a śārāp̄ — a fiery/serpent image — and mount it on a pole (nēs). Anyone who was bitten and looked at it would live (wāḥāy). The image of the snake that heals from snake-bites concentrates the serpent's danger and transforms it into healing; the form of judgment becomes the instrument of restoration. This structural reversal — the symbol of the curse becomes the cure — became foundational for Christological typology: the Christ who bore sin's curse becomes the healing for sin (John 3:14-15; 2 Cor 5:21).
9: Moses makes a bronze serpent (neḥaš nəḥōšet) — a pun in Hebrew: the word for snake (nāḥāš) and bronze (nəḥōšet) share the same root consonants, creating an auditory connection between the material and the object. The bronze serpent was later preserved in the Jerusalem temple, where it was worshipped as a cultic object called Nəḥuštān (2 Kgs 18:4). King Hezekiah destroyed it as part of his religious reforms — an interesting trajectory: a legitimate healing instrument became an object of illegitimate worship.
10: The wilderness itinerary resumes after the bronze serpent episode. The movement from Oboth toward the plains of Moab marks the final phase of the wilderness journey, transitioning from the post-Sinai law-giving and crisis period to the pre-conquest encampment east of the Jordan. Each station in the itinerary of vv10-20 represents the last legs of Israel's forty-year journey, with conquest imminent.
11: Iye-abarim in the wilderness facing Moab from the east — ʿal p̄ənê môʾāḇ mimizraḥ haššāmeš. The location places Israel in the Transjordanian wilderness east of Moab, moving northward along the Moabite border. The text preserves several station names unidentifiable in modern geography, reflecting authentic ancient itinerary records rather than composed theological geography.
12: The valley of Zered (naḥal zāred) is the major wadi marking the boundary between Edom and Moab (Deut 2:13-14 makes this crossing the significant marker of the completion of the forty years of wilderness wandering, as the last of the condemned generation has died by this point). The crossing of the Zered is thus structurally significant: it marks the generational transition from judgment to conquest.
13: The Arnon (naḥal ʾArnôn) was the major boundary river between Moab and the Amorite territory north of it. Israel encamps on the far (north) side of the Arnon, in the wilderness between Moab and the Amorites. This geographical precision sets up the diplomatic exchange with Sihon: Israel is in territory that Sihon controls, not Moab — so Moab's permission is not at issue for the next stage of the journey.
14: The Book of the Wars of YHWH (sep̄er milḥāmōt YHWH) — an ancient Israelite poetic anthology no longer extant, quoted here as a geographical authority. The existence of such a text, predating the final composition of Numbers, demonstrates that Numbers drew on older oral and written sources. Several scholars connect this book to the Song of Deborah (Judg 5) and other early Israelite war poetry preserved in fragments. The quotation here establishes Waheb in Suphah and the Arnon as recognized geographical markers from Israel's oldest literary traditions.
15: The continuation of the quotation locates the Arnon's slope leading to the settlement of Ar, resting on Moab's border. Ar was Moab's major city (Num 21:28; Isa 15:1; Deut 2:9). The ancient poetry functions as a geographical and historical marker — the Arnon as the acknowledged boundary — providing independent pre-narrative attestation of the territorial claims that follow in the Sihon and Og narratives.
16: Beer — bəʾēr — means simply Well. YHWH promises water here: ʾăsop̄â hāʿām wəʾetnâ lāhem māyim — assemble the people and I will give them water. This provision-from-the-well appears after the bronze serpent episode, possibly as an implicit contrast: where judgment-and-healing marked the serpent episode, here provision is straightforward. The well at Beer becomes the occasion for one of the oldest preserved songs in the Hebrew Bible.
17: The Song of the Well (vv17-18): ʿălî bəʾēr — spring up, O well — Israel's liturgical response to the gift of water. This is one of the most ancient poetic fragments preserved in Numbers, possibly from the same source tradition as the Book of the Wars of YHWH. The song addresses the well directly — a personification of the life-giving water that has been the subject of complaint and crisis throughout the wilderness narratives. Here, at the edge of the promised land, the well is cause for celebration rather than contention.
18: The princes (śārîm) dug the well with the scepter (məḥōqēq), nobles of the people with their own staffs (bəmiśʿenōtām). The imagery of leaders digging with ceremonial staffs may reflect the ANE practice of royal water-right ceremonies, where royalty formally inaugurated wells or irrigation projects. The well at Beer stands as a symbol of leadership provision at the threshold of the conquest — what princes provide in contrast to the murmuring people's self-provision.
19: The itinerary continues from Beer through Mattanah (gift), Nahaliel (wadi of God), and Bamoth (high places) — three station names that may have geographical significance (Nahaliel as a wadi, Bamoth as high-place sites) or may be symbolic of the ascent toward the heights of Moab and the Pisgah range from which the land will be surveyed.
20: Bamoth to the valley in Moab where Pisgah (hap̄isgāh) overlooks the wasteland (yāšîmōn). Pisgah is the mountain from which Balaam will bless Israel in ch23-24 and from which Moses will survey the promised land before his death (Deut 34:1 — the peak of Pisgah, identified with Nebo). The itinerary arrives at the geographical staging point for the rest of Numbers: the plains of Moab opposite Jericho, from which the conquest will be organized.
21: The diplomatic formula with Sihon king of the Amorites mirrors the Edom negotiation (20:14-17) almost exactly: messengers, request for passage on the King's Highway, promise not to enter fields or vineyards or drink well water. The parallel structure invites comparison: Edom's refusal was respected (Deut 2:4-6), while Sihon's refusal triggers military conflict. The difference: Edom is a brother nation protected from conquest by covenant; Sihon is an Amorite king whose land Israel was divinely destined to inherit.
22: The same terms as the Edom request: the King's Highway only, no agricultural encroachment, no water taken without payment. The repetition of the same diplomatic language shows that Israel approached Sihon in the same good faith as Edom. Deuteronomy 2:30 adds that YHWH hardened Sihon's spirit specifically to give Israel grounds to attack — making Sihon's refusal divinely determined within the conquest framework, as Pharaoh's hardening was within the exodus framework.
23: Sihon refuses passage and marches his forces into the wilderness to meet Israel at Jahaz. The refusal is identical in structure to Edom's (v18,20) but leads to a completely different outcome — not because Israel chose differently but because the conquest mandate applied to Amorite territory. Israel strikes Sihon's forces and takes possession of Amorite land from the Arnon to the Jabbok. The vocabulary (wayyiḵbōš — he took possession) introduces the conquest-language that dominates the rest of Numbers and all of Joshua.
24: Israel's northern boundary after Sihon's defeat: the Jabbok (naḥal yabbōq) — the river where Jacob wrestled with God (Gen 32:22), now the boundary of Israelite territory. The southern boundary is the Arnon, the northern boundary is the Jabbok (and the Ammonite border beyond). The phrase kî ʿazzûz gəḇûl bənê ʿammôn — for the border of the Ammonites was strong — explains why Israel stopped: Ammon was protected from conquest by the same covenant principle as Edom and Moab (Deut 2:19-23).
25: Israel settles in the Amorite towns, including Heshbon (ḥešbôn) and all its surrounding settlements. Heshbon becomes the royal capital of Israel's Transjordanian territory. The settlement of Amorite towns — rather than destruction — establishes the Transjordanian tribes' inheritance east of the Jordan, elaborated in Num 32 where Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh formally claim this territory.
26: Heshbon was Sihon's royal city; Sihon had previously taken it from the former Moabite king. The note that Sihon had conquered Moab is significant: Israel is not taking Moab's territory but Sihon's Amorite conquest territory. Deuteronomy 2:9 prohibits Israel from taking Moabite land; the clarification here explains why the conquest of Heshbon does not violate that prohibition. The legal-historical precision of the parenthetical note reflects P's concern for the legitimacy of territorial claims.
27: The taunt-song (hamoššəlîm — the poets/those who use proverbs) celebrates Sihon's original conquest of Moab: come to Heshbon, let Sihon's city be rebuilt. The quoted poetry is pre-Israelite — a Moabite lament for their own defeat by Sihon, which Israel now appropriates to mock Sihon. The use of enemy's own war poetry as the celebration of conquest represents a sophisticated literary and political appropriation of earlier cultural material.
28: The taunt-song imagery: fire coming out of Heshbon, a flame from Sihon's city devouring Ar of Moab. The fire/flame imagery describes Sihon's earlier conquest of Moab; now that same city is in Israelite hands, and the fire that once consumed Moab's defeat is itself consumed. The poetic justice is structural: Sihon's conquering fire is itself extinguished by a stronger force.
29: Woe to Moab — the taunt mourns the people of Chemosh (ʿam kəmôš). Chemosh was the national deity of Moab (1 Kgs 11:7,33; 2 Kgs 23:13); the reference uses the Moabite theological framework (their god failed to protect them from Sihon) against them. The phrase wayyittēn bānāyw p̄ēliṭim ûbənōtāyw baššibî — he gave his sons as fugitives and his daughters into captivity — describes Chemosh as having abandoned his people, a common ANE formulaic expression for a defeated nation's god.
30: Israel's own victory song supplements the Moabite taunt: we have devastated Heshbon as far as Dibon, destroyed as far as Nophah and Medeba. The geographical extent of destruction runs through Moabite-Sihonite territory. The quotation of multiple ancient poetry sources (Book of Wars, Moabite taunt-song, Israelite victory-song) within a single narrative unit gives the Sihon-Og conquest section exceptional literary density, marking it as a canonical high point in the wilderness narrative.
31: Israel settles in the land of the Amorites — the formal statement of possession that prepares for the Numbers 32 distribution narrative. The settlement precedes the Jordan crossing; the Transjordanian territory becomes a satellite of the conquest, an eastern wing of the promised land whose legitimacy depends on the Sihon victory narrated here and whose full history extends through the period of the Judges.
32: Jazer — a city in the Amorite territory north of Heshbon — is scouted and captured along with its surrounding settlements. The systematic expansion from Heshbon northward reflects the methodical nature of the Transjordanian campaign. Jazer later becomes a significant Gadite town (Num 32:1,3,35) and appears in the Levitical city lists (Josh 21:39).
33: Og king of Bashan comes out against Israel at Edrei. Bashan was the northern Transjordanian region, later notable for its rich agricultural land (oak forests, cattle; Ps 22:12; Amos 4:1). Og is repeatedly described as the last of the Rephaim — a giant or pre-Israelite race (Deut 3:11 mentions his oversized iron bed). The encounter with Og follows immediately on Sihon's defeat, giving the impression of rapid sequential conquest of the entire Transjordanian corridor.
34: YHWH's direct encouragement to Moses: ʾal-tîrāʾ ʾōtô — do not fear him. The fear-not formula appears throughout the conquest narratives (Josh 8:1; 10:8; 11:6) as the divine guarantee preceding battle. The formula kî bəyāḏekā nātatî ʾōtô — I have given him into your hand — declares the victory as accomplished before the battle. Doing exactly to Og what was done to Sihon is the divine mandate: the two kings are juridically equivalent, and their defeat together constitutes the complete pacification of Transjordan before the Jordan crossing.
35: The complete destruction of Og, his sons, and his whole army — leaving no survivors (wəlōʾ-šiʾēr lô śārîḏ) — completes the Transjordanian conquest. The phrase echoes throughout the conquest narratives in Joshua (Josh 8:22; 10:28,30,33,37,39; 11:8); the destruction formula becomes the literary marker of complete holy-war victory. Israel takes possession of Og's land, completing the territorial base from which the Jordan crossing and the Cisjordanian conquest will proceed. The two victory narratives (Sihon and Og) are quoted repeatedly in Deuteronomy-Psalms as paradigm events establishing YHWH's power for the conquest.
